responseSet.title: EDITORIAL
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

EDITORIAL

In this issue of the Journal is the winner of the 2006 Annual Clinical Essay Prize, awarded by the Educational Trust of the Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the NHS to John Wright's paper, ‘The remains of the day: Transsexuality, psychodynamics and the modern NHS’. As usual, the quality of the papers submitted for the prize was very high, and it was difficult to choose one paper over others. However, in the end the judges were unanimous, and the winner is to be congratulated for his beautifully written, well constructed paper which contains some original ideas, derived from psychotherapy with a transsexual in an NHS setting. The paper broadens current understanding of the psychodynamics of transsexuality and may also, hopefully, influence future practice in NHS transsexual services.

The closing date for next year's Essay Prize, which is open to members of the APP, is 31 December 2006. The winner will be announced at the 2007 Annual General Meeting of the APP (details about how to submit are on the inside back cover of this issue). I would, again, encourage APP members to finish that paper which you have drafted or presented, or which you may have constructed only in your mind, and submit it for the Essay Prize. I look forward to the very great pleasure I and the other members of the judging panel, and eventually our readers, will get from it.

Maureen Marks

Editor

139

responseSet.title: CONVERSATION ANALYSIS AND PSYCHOANALYTIC PSYCHOTHERAPY RESEARCH: QUESTIONS, ISSUES, PROBLEMS AND CHALLENGES
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

CONVERSATION ANALYSIS AND PSYCHOANALYTIC PSYCHOTHERAPY RESEARCH: QUESTIONS, ISSUES, PROBLEMS AND CHALLENGES

Michael Forrester and David Reason

David Reason

David Reason  

Understanding the talk of the ‘talking cure’ remains a central goal of researchers in psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Here, we consider whether conversation analysis (CA) can provide techniques to understand better the conduct of the psychoanalytic therapeutic interaction. Following discussion outlining the participant-oriented nature of this qualitative methodology we consider reasons for the emergence of CA-informed studies of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Amongst other aims, CA focuses on uncovering the process and procedures which make the therapy encounter a distinct form of ‘institutional life’. For psychoanalytically-oriented researchers, CA can refine their skills of attention and engender sensitivity to understanding material in sessions. Using examples from segments of talk between a training therapist and client we highlight both the advantages of, and constraints on, employing CA as an aid to understanding psychotherapeutic sessions by considering contrasting conceptions of temporality in conversation analysis and psychoanalysis. In the former participants are oriented towards the ongoing production of sequential understandings and local ‘context’ in an unfolding present, in the latter participants aim to enhance the emergence of the remote past into the present of the therapeutic interaction. While recognizing the research benefits of CA methodology concluding comments raise questions regarding the potential complementarity between our dispositions towards the close monitoring of the activity and the feelings of fellow humans.

responseSet.title: THE CONCEPT OF ‘PSYCHOLOGICAL SAFETY’ – A PSYCHOANALYTICALLY-INFORMED CONTRIBUTION TOWARDS ‘SAFE, SOUND & SUPPORTIVE’ MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

THE CONCEPT OF ‘PSYCHOLOGICAL SAFETY’ – A PSYCHOANALYTICALLY-INFORMED CONTRIBUTION TOWARDS ‘SAFE, SOUND & SUPPORTIVE’ MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES

Martin Seager

Martin Seager

Martin Seager  

It is not controversial to say that we live in an era of unprecedented preoccupation with the notion of ‘risk’ in our public mental health services, not to mention other areas of public life. Notions of ‘risk’ and ‘safety’ have long been at the heart of current government policy on mental health as epitomized by the guidance document entitled Modernising Mental Health Services: Safe, Sound & Supportive (Department of Health [DoH] 1998). This is partly a consequence of the continuing switch of emphasis by both major political parties over the last 20 years or more away from hospital or in-patient care and towards community-based services. This is not the place, however, to focus on the political context. However, in clinical terms what really is the difference between a ‘safe’ or ‘secure’ mental health service and an ‘unsafe’ or ‘insecure’ one? The more I have been invited to think about this question, the more complex and unfamiliar the territory has revealed itself to be.

responseSet.title: BOOK REVIEWS
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

BOOK REVIEWS

Mother–Infant Attachment and Psychoanalysis. The Eyes of Shame

by Mary Ayers (Brunner-Routledge, Hove & New York, 2003). 224 pp. £78.95 (hardback), £19.99 (paperback).

In this book Mary Ayers presents us with some valuable insights into the roots of shame from the perspective of the infant's experience of the mother. As a piece of personal analytic research it is a work of considerable scholarship. As a book for a readership attracted by the title Mother–Infant Attachment and Psychoanalysis, it is frustratingly sparing on clinical illustrations and did not, for me, establish a clear sense of the author's clinical practice and experiences. Ayers develops a theoretical thesis about the ‘Terrible Mother’ and the pervasively destructive impact of this Mother's cold, projective and unreflecting eye contact with her infant. This thesis is underpinned by a very extensive and rich literature search drawing upon a wide range of cultural, artistic and literary sources. Ayers defines the essential features of the psychic catastrophe that she is proposing in Chapter 3 as ‘a form of psychotic anxiety’ … (resulting) … ‘from a mother's unreflecting eyes; in other words, shame at its core’. The sentence which follows this identifies a principal concern of her thesis but also highlights a difficulty with it to some extent: ‘Shame is the annihilating affect that lies at the heart of a psychic element related to survival hidden in an otherwise sane personality’ (p. 74). In this sense Ayers is proposing a discrete and latent but nevertheless fundamental fracture in the psyche, originating at a very early or primitive stage of development. Although an arresting thought in terms of an individual psychopathology, she does not go on to provide compelling clinical evidence to illustrate her view of the problem in terms of the complexity of subsequent developmental processes of the individual, or from within the perspective of the mother–infant dyadic relationship.

responseSet.title: PSYCHOANALYSIS AND PSYCHOANALYTIC PSYCHOTHERAPY
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

PSYCHOANALYSIS AND PSYCHOANALYTIC PSYCHOTHERAPY

Christo Joannidis

Christo Joannidis

Christo Joannidis  

This paper attempts to address the similarities and differences between psychoanalytic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis as practice. Starting from the manifest technical points, which he dismisses as peripheral, the author reaches the position that he considers to be the fundamental area of contention, i.e. the tranference-countertransference arena. Focusing on the countertransference stance of the professional in this process, the author distinguishes the defining differences of these two approaches, and hopes to show them to be more complementary than antagonistic.

responseSet.title: BOOK REVIEW
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

BOOK REVIEW

Cutting it Out. A Journey through Psychotherapy and Self Harm

by Carolyn Smith (London & Philadelphia: Jessica Kingsley, 2006). 128 pp. £12.99 (paperback). ISBN 1-84310-266-8.

I was drawn to this book’s title because of the suggestion that it would be tracking treatment over time. I assumed that it was a academic book written by a psychotherapist and was surprised when I found that it is a semi-autobiographical novel written by a patient who has self-harmed.

The protagonist is a young woman who shares a flat with a girlfriend. She is attending therapy three times a week in North London. The story picks up the therapy after a year when the relationship with the therapist is vital to, and consuming of, the patient and her cutting is intertwined with the relationship. It vividly describes about a month of therapy. In this story it is the patient who speaks and who gives (fictional) verbatim extracts of sessions with her thoughts, feelings and reactions to the therapist and what has taken place between them.

As the reader I oscillated between being identified with the young woman and with her therapist. Equally I took a while to settle into this unsettling book, and repeatedly, just as I felt settled something would happen to unsettle me again. Both the powerful identification and the unsettled feelings are, I think, how it can feel being alongside someone who is desperately trying to express themselves but who is only just beginning to know there is someone who is listening to them and is thinking about them. The story makes explicit how the sharing and understanding of pain has a power to engage and hold the therapist and how when a turning point is reached it seems the patient elicits a response from the therapist that is made use of.

The story gives an excellent fictional account of how the words of the therapist are rearranged in the patient’s mind. It makes clear that a therapist has little option but to bide time, hope, and continue to offer understandings while knowing that what is offered may not be heard or may be given new meaning for better and for worse. Just when, as the reader, I was beginning to feel that nothing is going to make a difference, the patient does finally come to a turning point and begins to battle with the compulsion to cut and the cutting starts to lose its power.

responseSet.title: TOTALITARIANISM – THE INTERNAL WORLD AND THE POLITICAL MIND1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

TOTALITARIANISM – THE INTERNAL WORLD AND THE POLITICAL MIND1

Nick Temple

Nick Temple

Nick Temple  

This paper explores how totalitarianism can develop and become a stable structure both as a state of mind and as a political system. Using Shakespeare's Macbeth as an example the author draws attention to the universal nature of the temptations that power brings with it and which can lead to totalitarian domination. Aspects of Macbeth's mental life are compared with dictators such as Hitler and Stalin. A clinical example is then given which further illustrates and explores the phenomenon of totalitarian ways of thinking as encountered in the consulting room showing how individuals can assert their power through the use of omnipotent destructiveness. This type of destructiveness is more effective when the patient is freed from guilt and the mental mechanisms of psychic retreats are used to maintain their totalitarian systems.

responseSet.title: THE OEDIPUS COMPLEX IN ANOREXIA NERVOSA
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

THE OEDIPUS COMPLEX IN ANOREXIA NERVOSA

Anthony Winston

Anthony Winston

Anthony Winston  

Failure to work through the Oedipus complex may be central to some cases of anorexia nervosa. The negative Oedipus complex seems to be of particular importance in this disorder and inability to work through the negative complex may lead to a premature move to the positive complex. In anorexia, the Oedipus complex may manifest itself as an enmeshed relationship with one parent and the exclusion of the other. This deprives the patient of the opportunity to experience frustration and fantasies of omnipotence are perpetuated. The patient is unable to experience ambivalence and cannot progress from the paranoid-schizoid to the depressive position. Maintenance of therapeutic boundaries provides a challenge to the patient's omnipotence, which allows a shift to the depressive position and the development of a sense of separateness. Case vignettes are used to

1. Throughout this paper, Oedipal dynamics are explored from the perspective of the female child. This reflects the fact that the majority of anorexics are female. Consequently, I have referred to the anorexic patient as female and used the pronoun ‘she’. Oedipal dynamics are, of course, important in male anorexics too but they are significantly different from those in girls and beyond the scope of this paper.2. In order to protect the privacy of patients, the two cases described are fictional. However, they are based closely on clinical experience and illustrate therapeutic issues that have arisen in work with real patients. Readers will be able to judge for themselves whether these vignettes reflect their own experience of working with anorexic patients.1

illustrate these processes in both positive and negative forms of the Oedipus complex.

responseSet.title: GLANCING OVER THE SHOULDER: RACISM, FEAR OF THE STRANGER AND THE FASCIST STATE OF MIND
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

GLANCING OVER THE SHOULDER: RACISM, FEAR OF THE STRANGER AND THE FASCIST STATE OF MIND

Julian Lousada

Julian Lousada

Julian Lousada  

The first time one is called nigger, or told to go back where you come from, one's identity is transduced and a great violence is done to one's self-esteem. Thereafter one fights a rear-guard action to keep other elements of oneself in focus. It is hard to get through the day without the shoulder coming into play, I don't mean the chip on the shoulder, I mean the glance over the shoulder. Once somebody has mounted a stealth attack on a part of who you are you had better be on your guard for you know it is coming again. Phillips (2004).

responseSet.title: Book reviews
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book reviews

A Short Introduction to Psychoanalysis by Jane Milton, Caroline Polmear and Julia Fabricius (Sage Publications, London, 2004). 192 pp.

A Short Introduction to Psychoanalysis is co-edited by three London-based psychoanalysts, Jane Milton (previously editor of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy), Caroline Polmear and Julia Fabricius. It is part of the ‘Short Introductions to the Therapy Professions’ series, from Sage Publications, and in less than 200 pages the authors endeavour to offer ‘a down-to-earth guide to arguably the most misunderstood of all the psychological therapies’. This is a huge task and for the most part the authors succeed in their task.

The first two chapters ‘What is Psychoanalysis?’ and ‘Basic Tenets’ offer a concise and condensed guide through many of the basic concepts and tenets, with the text nicely punctuated by case examples and explanations. The authors then move on to broader issues, such as the history of psychoanalysis, psychoanalysis across cultures, critiques of psychoanalysis, research in psychoanalysis, psychoanalysis and psychotherapy, applications of psychoanalysis, and finally ‘The Profession’.

These two first chapters are highly condensed. Concepts such as transference, countertransference, containment and defence mechanisms are defined and explained, as are the core psychoanalytic theories ranging from Freud's developmental stages to Klein's positions, and the Winnicottian facilitating environment. Interwoven with these explanations are vignettes from the treatment or assessment of patients, elucidating or illustrating the concepts in the text. This works well. For the most part these two chapters ‘worked’, and if they were co-authored, they appeared for the most part ‘seamless’.

Chapter 3 provides a well written and interesting history of psychoanalysis, including the life and works of Freud, and is followed by the history of some of the important figures who either remained loyal or who broke with Freud (e.g. Adler and Jung). A further six pages are dedicated to an interesting annotated listing of ‘key historical figures’, analysts ranging from Ernest Jones to Joseph Sandler, who have made substantial contributions to the theory and practice of psychoanalysis, with a British bias.

					<artauth hidden="false"><aut alias="false" role="author" listed="true" authindexid="Caparrotta, Luigi"><nfirst>Luigi</nfirst><nlast>Caparrotta</nlast></aut> <aut alias="false" role="author" listed="true" authindexid="Ghaffari, Kamran"><nfirst>Kamran</nfirst><nlast>Ghaffari</nlast></aut> </artauth>

responseSet.title: A HISTORICAL OVERVIEW OF THE PSYCHODYNAMIC CONTRIBUTIONS TO THE UNDERSTANDING OF EATING DISORDERS
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A HISTORICAL OVERVIEW OF THE PSYCHODYNAMIC CONTRIBUTIONS TO THE UNDERSTANDING OF EATING DISORDERS

Luigi Caparrotta and Kamran Ghaffari

Kamran Ghaffari

Kamran Ghaffari  

The objective of this paper is to give an historical overview of the development of psychodynamic theories and to acknowledge their contribution to the understanding of the aetiology of these complex disorders. Relevant psychodynamic and psychoanalytic papers on the historical development of eating disorders were identified through a search of (1) Medline and PsychoInfo, (2) the library of the London Institute of Psychoanalysis, (3) Psychoanalytic Electronic Publishing (PEP), and (4) a number of textbooks on eating disorders.

The search revealed a dearth of psychoanalytic historical perspectives about the aetiology of eating disorders in general. Overall most psychoanalytic publications were based on single case studies and concerned primarily with anorexia nervosa. No single model was identified, but a number of psychoanalytic ideas remain consistent throughout. Several psychoanalysts and contemporary psychodynamically-informed authors refer to, apply and have further developed a number of these original ideas. This overview highlights the unique contributions of psychodynamic concepts towards a better understanding of the aetiology of eating disorders and how much they have influenced and informed modern thinking on the meaning of these complex conditions. A range of psychodynamic views has emerged and gradually evolved from concentrating solely on intra-psychic factors to a more comprehensive multi-modal model. This tendency has become a fertile ground for cross-fertilization which, along with the bio-social counterparts, remains the fundamental pillars on which modern understanding of eating disorders still rests.

responseSet.title: SUICIDALITY IN MEN IN PSYCHODYNAMIC PSYCHOTHERAPY
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

SUICIDALITY IN MEN IN PSYCHODYNAMIC PSYCHOTHERAPY

Reinhard Lindner

Reinhard Lindner

Reinhard Lindner  

Based on the evidence of empirical gender differences in attempted suicide and suicide, this paper develops psychodynamic hypotheses on gender-specific biographic, intra-psychic and treatment aspects of suicidality among men. ‘Forming Types by Understanding’ is a qualitative method, based on a systematic analysis of similarity and differential reference points to develop ideal types of suicidal men. A random sample of 20 suicidal men who were receiving out-patient psychodynamic psychotherapy was studied.

Four ideal types of suicidal men were identified: (1) ideal type ‘disconnected’: a predominance of disconnected feelings that is associated with rejection experiences; (2) ideal type ‘hurt’: aggressive conflicts and the realization of disillusioned life reality combined with experiences of real loss and real trauma; (3) ideal type ‘stormy’: an attached-symbiotic transference offer mobilizes unrealistic wishes to help against a background of unresolved dependency conflicts; and (4) ideal type ‘object dependent’: concrete facts are important in relationships; in case of separation, real repossession of the woman must occur, a lack of emotional experience is compensated. The danger of suicidality, especially in the early stage of psychotherapy, is particularly acute for patients who cannot reach their therapists emotionally and vice versa. Disturbance of the early experience of inseparability from the mother, gender-specific conflicts in the development of separation and individuation, and failed triangulation are of central psychodynamic importance.

responseSet.title: Book Reviews
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book Reviews

The Troubled Mind of Northern Ireland: An Analysis of the Emotional Effects of the Troubles

Edited by Raman Kapur and Jim Campbell (Karnac, London, 2004), 156 pp. £16.99 (paperback).

In the aftermath of the deadly attacks of 11th September 2001, a number of authors have considered the nature of terrorism and political violence from a psychoanalytic perspective (Covington et al. 2002, Varvin and Volkan 2003). This international focus has brought into sharp relief the relative neglect within the British and Irish psychoanalytic literature of the most recent period of bloody conflict centred in Northern Ireland. With a few notable exceptions (Benson 1994, Kapur 2001, Alderdice 2005, Elliott 2005), authors within Northern Ireland itself have appeared reluctant, if not resistant, to engage with the explosive psychodynamics of the Troubles – itself a euphemistic term that understates the scale and intensity of the associated terror and suffering.

In response to an invitation to address a recent conference on the enduring psychological impact of the Troubles, a highly respected and very experienced psychotherapist, based a short distance outside Belfast, commented that little if any psychological fallout from the conflict seems to emerge as material within the context of private psychotherapy sessions. One of the aims of the present volume is to challenge this complacent notion that the Troubles happened elsewhere and belong to a different social class. Drawing on Kapur and Campbell's respective backgrounds in psychoanalytic psychotherapy and social work, this collaboration offers a psycho-social perspective on the widespread psychological effects of a conflict that is deeply rooted, but often only understood at a surface level.

A more thoughtful analysis is set out here, which challenges the powerful myth of two religious tribes warring with one another while a benign state attempts to keep the peace. It is made uncomfortably clear that the political violence that resulted in the deaths of over 3600 human beings took place at both an inter-communal and a state level. At the same time, while outlining the regressive influence on Northern Irish society of fundamentalist religious ideology, the authors also emphasize the significance of an underlying social class dimension.

responseSet.title: CHANGE IN SELF-IMAGE AND PTSD SYMPTOMS IN SHORT-TERM THERAPIES WITH TRAUMATIZED REFUGEES
responseSet.authorMast: Rolf Holmqvist,  Kjell Andersen,  Tanvir Anjum and  Birgitta Alinder
responseSet.authorCitation: Holmqvist, R., Andersen, K., Anjum, T. & Alinder, B.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

CHANGE IN SELF-IMAGE AND PTSD SYMPTOMS IN SHORT-TERM THERAPIES WITH TRAUMATIZED REFUGEES

Rolf Holmqvist , Kjell Andersen , Tanvir Anjum and Birgitta Alinder

Birgitta Alinder

Birgitta Alinder  

In this study, an attempt was made to evaluate changes in the self-image of traumatized refugees receiving short-term psychotherapy. Fourteen clients with war and torture experiences were followed during and after trauma-focused therapies with self-rating instruments. Outcome was measured with SCL-90, measuring overall psychiatric symptoms, and with PTSS-10, measuring Post-traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) symptoms. Self-image was measured with Structural Analysis of Social Behavior (SASB). The analyses showed considerable remission of both general psychiatric and PTSD-related symptoms, but more moderate changes in self-image aspects. The positive aspect of the self-image was virtually non-related to the symptom measures and did not change after the termination of the therapy; the negative aspect of the self-image was more strongly related to the symptoms and changed mainly on the last measurement occasion, 15 months after the therapy end. When clients with good and poor outcome were compared, it was found that those with good outcome had higher initial levels on both the positive and the negative aspects of the self-images. This was tentatively interpreted as indicating that clients who had invested more energy in their self-image were better able to utilize the therapy. Different development in the self-image for these two groups indicated that for some clients, the treatment process might have entailed too much strain.

responseSet.title: TRANSFERENCE-FOCUSED PSYCHOTHERAPY AND MENTALIZATION-BASED TREATMENT: BROTHER AND SISTER?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

TRANSFERENCE-FOCUSED PSYCHOTHERAPY AND MENTALIZATION-BASED TREATMENT: BROTHER AND SISTER?

Annelies Verheugt-Pleiter and Margit Deben-Mager

Margit Deben-Mager

Margit Deben-Mager  

Transference-focused psychotherapy and mentalization-based treatment are new psychoanalytic treatment forms for borderline patients. How do these forms of treatment differ and how are they alike? What interventions do they yield in clinical practice? In the past few years two methods of psychoanalytic treatment for borderline patients have been developed: transference-focused therapy and mentalization-based treatment. This paper explores the similarities and differences between them, with a special focus on how the different theories lead to different interventions in clinical practice. TFP takes the central problem to be the disorder in object relations, while MBT focuses on the self as agent. Further differences concern notions of the role of aggression, the presence of mental representations and the position of the therapist. Interventions formulated by therapists of both frames of reference in response to some therapy fragments differed substantially. Both theories share a desire to develop a psychoanalytical technique suitable for borderline patients, and both stress the central importance of the handling of the transference and of working in the here-and-now, as well as the necessity for effect research.

					<artauth hidden="false"><aut alias="false" role="author" listed="true" authindexid="Hinshelwood, Robert. D."><nfirst>Robert. D.</nfirst><nlast>Hinshelwood</nlast></aut> </artauth>

responseSet.title: RACISM: BEING IDEAL
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

RACISM: BEING IDEAL

Robert. D. Hinshelwood

Robert. D. Hinshelwood

Robert. D. Hinshelwood  

‘Race’ is both an empty category and one of the most destructive and powerful forms of social categorization. How is this paradox to be explained and how are its negative consequences for human lives to be resisted? (Rustin 1991 : 57).

Psychoanalysis is limited in what it can say about social categories. It can say something in general about the process of categorizing. Categorizing is a ubiquitous process, which is both a powerful tool for civilization and also one of the most destructive forces of oppression. The way social categories are used is a social phenomenon. However, in considering race as one example of these destructive forms of stereotyping, I shall argue that the organization of the individual mind is a basis on which the organization of social categories necessarily depends. I shall detail the model of racism which Freud hinted at, and then use racism as one example of the destructive and narcissistic form of stereotyping.

					<artauth hidden="false"><aut alias="false" role="author" listed="true" authindexid="Janes, Emma Sarah"><nfirst>Emma Sarah</nfirst><nlast>Janes</nlast></aut> </artauth>

responseSet.title: ‘QUIS CUSTODIET?’: WHO HAS ULTIMATE RESPONSIBILITY FOR CONFIDENTIALITY IN MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘QUIS CUSTODIET?’: WHO HAS ULTIMATE RESPONSIBILITY FOR CONFIDENTIALITY IN MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES?

Emma Sarah Janes

Emma Sarah Janes

Emma Sarah Janes  

Confidentiality is a complex and engaging subject. One of the questions I would ask the reader to consider is whether ‘absolute’ confidentiality can truly exist within the analytic relationship and whether it is worth asking the same question in relation to psychotherapy as it is practised within the NHS. Is the position of absolute confidence a noble aspiration or could it be regarded as a rather inflexible stance whose advocates occupy an arrogant and even omnipotent position? This last question deliberately invokes a false dichotomy and I would argue that the clinical reality of working with patients continually dispels the myth that ‘absolute’ confidentiality can exist within the analytic relationship. If we can accept this position, then the stage is set for a potentially more fruitful, if complex, debate about where the line of disclosure lies for individual cases. It would seem timely, in view of the recent publication of the Royal College of Psychiatrists' report Good Psychiatric Practice: Confidentiality & Information Sharing (2006), to reflect upon how the ‘contract’ of confidentiality is drawn up in the apparently dyadic relationship of analytic therapy as practised in the private sector and to consider the particular conflicts and threats to patient confidence posed by the multidisciplinary and highly transparent ways of working that have evolved within modern NHS mental health services.

responseSet.title: THE REMAINS OF THE DAY: TRANSSEXUALITY, PSYCHODYNAMICS AND THE MODERN NHS
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

THE REMAINS OF THE DAY: TRANSSEXUALITY, PSYCHODYNAMICS AND THE MODERN NHS

John Wright

John Wright

John Wright  

This paper describes a psychodynamic understanding of transsexualism and some related implications for NHS services. Following a brief literature review the author describes a series of clinical vignettes that tease out psychically important transsexual dilemmas. Specifically, themes of conflict with authority, idealization of destructive solutions and learning to live with regret are explored. Finally a psychological differentiation of transsexual issues is suggested as potentially helpful for future service development.

responseSet.title: MAKING ROOM FOR MADNESS IN MENTAL HEALTH: THE IMPORTANCE OF ANALYTICALLY-INFORMED SUPERVISION OF NURSES AND OTHER MENTAL HEALTH PROFESSIONALS
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

MAKING ROOM FOR MADNESS IN MENTAL HEALTH: THE IMPORTANCE OF ANALYTICALLY-INFORMED SUPERVISION OF NURSES AND OTHER MENTAL HEALTH PROFESSIONALS

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans  

In this paper, I discuss the importance of psychoanalytically informed supervision and training of nurses and other professionals in mental health settings. Using examples from supervision groups I will illustrate how a psychoanalytic approach to mental health can complement other ways of thinking about practice and management. Psychoanalysis provides a framework for understanding the relationship between the internal world of the patients, the therapeutic relationship and the social system. I will also argue that this understanding helps to develop and maintain therapeutic factors while reducing the risk of damaging behaviour and beliefs.

responseSet.title: HOMELESSNESS AND CONTAINMENT – A PSYCHOTHERAPY PROJECT WITH HOMELESS PEOPLE AND WORKERS IN THE HOMELESS FIELD
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

HOMELESSNESS AND CONTAINMENT – A PSYCHOTHERAPY PROJECT WITH HOMELESS PEOPLE AND WORKERS IN THE HOMELESS FIELD

June Campbell

June Campbell

June Campbell  

A high percentage of homeless people suffer from a severe degree of personality disorder that causes them to fall through the net of psychiatric and caring services. Their persistent inability to maintain an actual dwelling-place as a location of stability and meaning, is also reflected in an inner state of fragility, and in relationship difficulties that lead to destructive behaviours that inevitably alienate others. In this paper, the relationship between homelessness, the maternal body, buildings and containment is explored, using clinical material which illustrates how the claustro-agoraphobic dilemma (Henri Rey 1994), intrinsic to the state of homelessness and to borderline conditions, impacts on vulnerable individuals and on care workers in this field. Examples drawn from staff consultations demonstrate how psychodynamic thinking can help to provide containment within systems of care.

responseSet.title: SEPTEMBER 111
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

SEPTEMBER 111

Hanna Segal

Hanna Segal

Hanna Segal  

In this paper Segal gives brief summaries of her 1987 paper ‘Silence is the real crime’ and her 1995 paper ‘From Hiroshima to the Gulf War and after: socio-political expressions of ambivalence’, to show the effect of psychotic factors in the background to September 11. She analyses the symbolic significance and psychological impact of September 11 and in a 2005 Postscript discusses the ensuing war on Iraq.

responseSet.title: CONSIDERATION OF THE ROLE OF PSYCHOTHERAPY IN REDUCING THE RISK OF SUICIDE IN AFFECTIVE DISORDERS – A CASE STUDY
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

CONSIDERATION OF THE ROLE OF PSYCHOTHERAPY IN REDUCING THE RISK OF SUICIDE IN AFFECTIVE DISORDERS – A CASE STUDY

Caroline Taylor-Thomas and Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas  

In this paper material is presented from a patient with a diagnosis of a recurrent affective disorder and exhibiting resistance to engaging in the work of therapy alongside the emergence of active suicidal intent. Supervision can help in containing intensely disturbing feelings in the therapist and aid in identifying the underlying psychosis. Through exploration of the counter-transference feelings, the therapist can become attuned to a playing down of the psychosis by the patient and alert other involved professionals. Technically, the challenge remains one of how to make an impact in the sessions through converting a psychotic monologue into a dialogue.

responseSet.title: THE TOTALITARIAN STATE OF MIND
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

THE TOTALITARIAN STATE OF MIND

Eileen McGinley and Arturo Varchevker

Arturo Varchevker

Arturo Varchevker  

INTRODUCTION

This special edition of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy draws on three public events organized by the Psychoanalytic Forum of the British Psychoanalytic Society on the topic of Totalitarian States of Mind. The aim of these events was to explore the human factors that contribute to the development of oppressive states and societies and how this links with oppressive and destructive states of mind. The way in which certain mental processes manifest and get supported within various social contexts to produce totalitarian systems raises significant questions.

The term totalitarian means ‘relating to a polity’, that is, an organized society or state that permits no rival loyalties or parties. Totalitarianism includes the notion of oppressive, one-party politics that exist and are maintained through tyrannous and dictatorial means. Totalitarianism thrives on a belief system that offers omnipotence and projection of hated aspects of the self and one's allies onto another group. This definition in itself already resonates with psychoanalytic ideas on pathological organizations of the personality and destructive narcissism and the destructive and despotic nature of these states of mind if they remain unchallenged and inaccessible to reason or judgement.

The publication of these essays is a contribution to the discourse concerning the development of totalitarianism and offers an understanding of intrapsychic and unconscious elements within the individual's personality that also take into account sociological and historical perspectives. All the contributors explicitly or implicitly recognize that totalitarianism is a phenomenon that has as its characteristics the quality of universality in human history. For this reason all four authors consider aspects of totalitarianism in the social domain, but also explore in what way the understanding of individual mental functioning from a psychoanalytic perspective can further the understanding of this phenomenon.

responseSet.title: PREDICTORS OF ENGAGEMENT IN PSYCHODYNAMIC PSYCHOTHERAPY DERIVING FROM PRE-ASSESSMENT QUESTIONNAIRES
responseSet.authorMast: Maria Parissis,  Rob Whitley and  Anne Ward
responseSet.authorCitation: Parissis, M., Whitley, R. & Ward, A.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

PREDICTORS OF ENGAGEMENT IN PSYCHODYNAMIC PSYCHOTHERAPY DERIVING FROM PRE-ASSESSMENT QUESTIONNAIRES

Maria Parissis , Rob Whitley and Anne Ward

Anne Ward

Anne Ward  

Patients attending the Maudsley Hospital Psychotherapy Unit complete a semi-structured questionnaire as a matter of standard procedure prior to assessment. Clinical experience suggests that this instrument reveals important aspects of a patient's internal world, regularly borne out at the assessment interview. The questionnaire may thus contribute to predicting engagement in the therapeutic relationship. To evaluate this hypothesis we conducted a qualitative analysis of 40 questionnaires, 20 of patients who attended 20 sessions or more, the other 20 of patients who discontinued at or before the sixth session. We differentially explored characteristics that may indicate the probability of patients' engagement and, hence, help to predict outcome in terms of completion of the treatment. The results indicate that there are no strong discriminating factors discernable in the questionnaires for the prediction of engagement in psychotherapy. Two themes emerged from the analysis that seemed to have a moderate bearing on completion. These are the willingness of patients to observe themselves reflectively as well as the awareness that psychotherapy might be a difficult but necessary and beneficial undertaking.
